Iowa data on violent crime, arrests, and prison population

Violent crime in Iowa

In 2021, 206 of 276 law enforcement agencies in Iowa reported crime data to the FBI, covering 92 percent of the state’s population.

A list of law enforcement agencies in Iowa that did not fully report to the FBI on crime in 2021 can be found at the bottom of this page.

  • All violent crime
  • Homicide
  • Aggravated assault
  • Robbery
  • Rape
  • By offense
Background on crime data reporting

Beginning with data year 2021, the FBI discontinued collecting Uniform Crime Reporting data via the Summary Reporting System and required law enforcement agencies to use the National Incident-Based Reporting System (NIBRS) to report crime statistics to the FBI. Because many states did not have full NIBRS reporting coverage in 2021, the FBI and Bureau of Justice Statistics generated estimated crime statistics based on the agencies that reported data to the FBI. The reported crime and clearance rates for 2021 have a level of uncertainty represented by a margin of error for each estimate because not all agencies reported data to the FBI.

Arrests for violent crime in Iowa

In 2021, 54 percent (±2.2) of violent crimes reported to police were cleared by arrest or exceptional means.1 This is 3 percentage points lower than the clearance rate in 2010.

  • All violent crime
  • By offense

Prison population in Iowa

There were 2,934 people in prison for violent offenses in at the end of 2020, comprising 35 percent of the total prison population. Since 2010, the number of people in prison for violent offenses decreased by 10 percent. During that same period, the number of people in prison for nonviolent offenses decreased by 13 percent. Data on prison population trends in 2021 and 2022 are not available in national datasets, but can be analyzed upon request.

  • Violent vs. nonviolent
  • By offense

Law enforcement agencies not reporting to NIBRS

Not all law enforcement agencies have begun reporting NIBRS data to the FBI. As of data year 2021, 206 of 276 agencies in Iowa reported NIBRS data, covering 91 percent of the state’s population. Agencies that did not report in 2021 are listed in the table below. The population column is the number of residents of the jurisdiction covered by the law enforcement agency.2

Data sources

Crime and clearance rates

Data regarding crime and clearance rates come from the FBI’s Uniform Crime Reporting Program. State-level counts of reported violent crime between 2010 and 2020 are derived from the Summary Reporting System (SRS) in which law enforcement agencies submitted monthly counts of the four violent Part 1 Index Crimes (murder, aggravated assault, rape, and robbery). The FBI generated state-level estimates for reported crime from 1979 to 2020 based on agency-level reports, and these estimates are used on this page for counts of crime. These estimates can be downloaded from the FBI’s Crime Data Explorer.

State-level crime rates are calculated by dividing the number of estimated offenses by the total state population. Population data come from the U.S. Census Bureau American Community Survey 1-year estimates (table B01003) and the 2020 Decennial Census Redistricting Data (table P1).

State-level clearance rates are estimated by aggregating agency-level data from the SRS Offenses Known and Clearances by Arrest (Return A) dataset. The clearance rates reported here are the number of offenses cleared by arrest plus the number of offenses exceptionally cleared divided by the total number of offenses known.

National rates of crime and clearances come from the FBI’s annual report, Crime in the U.S.

State-level crime and clearance data for 2021 come from the FBI’s National Incident-Based Reporting System state estimation program. These estimates can be downloaded from the FBI’s Crime Data Explorer. Crime data for 2021 are included in these pages for states in which law enforcement agencies covering at least 90 percent of the population reported NIBRS data.

NIBRS participation

The number of agencies reporting to NIBRS comes from the the State level UCR Participation Endpoint API. The participation status of individual agencies comes from the Agency level UCR Participation Endpoint API. Documentation of the FBI crime data API endpoints can be found on Crime Data Explorer.

Prison population

State-level total prison populations come from the Bureau of Justice Statistics Prisoners series reports, which publishes year-end population counts for each state’s prison system. The data in these annual reports come from state reports to BJS via the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) and the National Prisoner Statistics Program.

NCRP data were used to calculate the percentage of people in prison for violent and non-violent offenses, as well as by violent offense type. NCRP data are collected annually from state departments of correction, which report individual-level data on people in prison custody. Not all states report NCRP data to BJS each year.

To generate the proportions of people in prison for different offense categories, annual NCRP data were filtered to remove people with prison sentences of less than one year. An offense is associated with each person’s prison term, and this offense was used to calculate prison population by offense. The violent offense category includes murder, manslaughter, rape, robbery, aggravated or simple assault, and other violent offenses.

The total prison populations from the BJS Prisoner series reports were combined with the percentages calculated using NCRP data to impute the counts of prison population by the offense categories shown on this page.

Alaska, Connecticut, Delaware, Hawaii, Rhode Island, and Vermont have integrated systems that combine prisons and jails, so the populations reported to NCRP include people held in both prisons and jails.

NCRP data can be downloaded from the National Archive of Criminal Justice Data.

Footnotes

  1. An incident is considered to be cleared by exceptional means if a law enforcement agency is unable to arrest or formally charge a person with a crime, but the agency has met the following conditions:

    1. Identified the person who committed the crime
    2. Gathered enough evidence to support an arrest, make a charge, and turn over the person to the court for prosecution
    3. Identified the suspect’s exact location so that the person could be taken into custody immediately
    4. Encountered a circumstance outside the control of law enforcement that prohibits the agency from arresting, charging, and prosecuting the person

    Examples of exceptional clearances include the death of the person who committed the crime; the victim’s refusal to cooperate with the prosecution after the person who committed the crime has been identified; or the denial of extradition.↩︎

  2. Some agencies, such as university and park police, are considered to have a population of zero by the FBI.↩︎
